subject: Statistics
title: The Boy or Girl Paradox
description: A micro-lesson that makes The Boy or Girl Paradox usable.

hook.text: A family has two children. One is a boy. What's the probability the other is also a boy? Not 1/2.

buildup.text: Two children create four equally likely outcomes: BB, BG, GB, GG. Knowing one is a boy eliminates only GG.

discovery.text: Three outcomes remain: BB, BG, GB. Only one has two boys. The probability is 1/3, not 1/2.

twist.text: But if you know the older child is a boy, it's 1/2. How you learn the information changes the probability.

climax.text: The paradox reveals that probability depends on the exact question asked. Phrasing is everything.

punchline.text: 'One is a boy' and 'the first is a boy' give different answers.

quiz.question: In the boy or girl paradox, why is the answer 1/3 instead of 1/2?
options: Knowing 'at least one is a boy' leaves three equally likely outcomes, and only one is two boys
Boys are statistically rarer than girls
The paradox only works with large families
